---
title: "Parallel statistical computing in Python on greatlakes for STATS 531 W26"
author: "Edward Ionides"
format:
  beamer:
    slide-level: 2
    header-includes: |
      \setbeamertemplate{footline}[page number]
    output-file: slides.pdf
    classoption: t
  pdf:
    header-includes: |
    output-file: notes.pdf

jupyter: python3

---

## Requirements to log in to greatlakes

We follow the [Great Lakes online documentation](https://its.umich.edu/advanced-research-computing/high-performance-computing/great-lakes/getting-started). You need:

- **A Slurm account**. Everybody in this class is a member of the account `stats531w26s001_class`. Graduate students in the Applied Statistics and Data Science masters programs, or Statistics PhD program, also have a primary departmental account, `stats_dept1`. 
- **A greatlakes cluster login account**. If you have not yet filled in the form at <https://its.umich.edu/advanced-research-computing/high-performance-computing/great-lakes/getting-started>
then do so.
- **A umich internet address**. Use the umich VPN if you are not on campus.


## Connecting to greatlakes with macOS, Linux or Windows

1. Open a Terminal window (recall that, on a Mac, this can be done using Control-Spacebar and typing Terminal) and type

```bash
ssh uniqname@greatlakes.arc-ts.umich.edu
```

where `uniqname` is your uniqname.

2. Login with your Kerberos level-1 password, and Okta two-factor authentication.

This creates a remote terminal shell on greatlakes.


## Connecting to greatlakes with a browser

* Use the umich VPN if you are not on campus.

* Point your browser to \url{https://greatlakes.arc-ts.umich.edu}

* Choose the menu option: Clusters $\rightarrow$ Great Lakes Shell Access. This creates a remote terminal shell on greatlakes within your browser.


## Moving files on and off greatlakes: `scp`

`scp` which has similar syntax to the terminal command `cp`.
To copy `myfile` on your laptop to a subdirectory `mydir` of your home directory on greatlakes:

\small
```bash
scp myfile uniqname@greatlakes-xfer.arc-ts.umich.edu:mydir
```
\normalsize

To copy an entire directory, use the `-r` flag for recursive copy:

\small
```bash
scp -r mydir uniqname@greatlakes-xfer.arc-ts.umich.edu:
```
\normalsize

These commands can also be reversed to copy files from greatlakes to your machine. To copy `mydir` back to your machine:

\small
```bash
scp -r uniqname@greatlakes-xfer.arc-ts.umich.edu:mydir .
```
\normalsize

You will need to authenticate via Okta to complete the file transfer.
On Mac or Windows, [FileZilla](https://filezilla-project.org) provides a file system user interface.


## Synchronizing files using git

* If you have a project on GitHub, clone the git repository to greatlakes and push your work back to greatlakes when you are done.

* This workflow keeps your project synchronized between greatlakes, GitHub, and your laptop.


## A workflow for working with batch jobs

1. You create a batch script and submit it as a job
2. Your job is scheduled, and it enters the queue
3. When its turn arrives, your job will execute the batch script
4. Your script has access to all applications and data
5. When your script completes, anything it sent to standard output and error are saved in files stored in your submission directory
6. You can ask that email be sent to you when your jobs starts, ends, or fails
7. You can check on the status of your job at any time, or delete it if it's not doing what you want
8. A short time after your job completes, it disappears


## Useful batch commands

Submit a job

```bash
sbatch sample.sbat
```

Query job status

```bash
squeue -j jobid
squeue -u uniqname
```

Delete a job

```bash
scancel jobid
```

Check a job script and estimate its start time

```bash
sbatch --test-only sample.sbat
```

## More Slurm commands to try

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `sacct -u user` | show recent job history |
| `seff jobid` | show cpu utilization for jobid |
| `my_accounts` | list accounts you have permission to use |


## Python modules on greatlakes

Software on greatlakes is packaged in modules which must be loaded

```bash
module load python
```

Other versions of Python are available:

```bash
module avail python
```

- For simple multicore computing, sending jobs to multiple cores on a single node, the default Python module is appropriate.


## A test for multiprocessing

- The `greatlakes` subdirectory of the `531w26` git repository has a file `test.sbat` which submits a batch job running the parallel multiprocessing test in `test.py`.

- A basic Linux exercise is to set up a directory on greatlakes with these files, at which point you can run

```bash
sbatch test.sbat
```

to submit the job. If you have little or no experience with Linux or Unix, this is a nontrivial task. You could ask for help or read <https://ubuntu.com/tutorials/command-line-for-beginners>.

- You can transfer the files from your laptop via `scp`, or by copy-paste, but it may be simplest to clone the class git repository into your greatlakes account,

```bash
git clone https://github.com/ionides/531w26.git
```

## Editing text files on greatlakes

- Inspect the text file `test.sbat`, for example by

```bash
more test.sbat
```

Is it fairly self-explanatory?

- One thing that needs changing is to set your email address for alerts about jobs beginning and ending.
- To make these edits on greatlakes, you need a text editor.
- It is convenient to use a text editor that runs in a terminal. Options include

```bash
vi test.sbat
emacs -nw test.sbat
nano test.sbat
```

- It is useful to acquire some familiarity with each of these editors.


## Comparing results

- You are now ready to run a batch job

```bash
sbatch test.sbat
```

- From inspecting the code in `test.py`, we see that the results are saved in `test.csv`

- You can assess what you learn from comparing run times on greatlakes and your laptop, though the main goal here is just to practice running the code.


## Python packages on greatlakes

- After making Python available by running

```bash
module load python
```

you can start a Python session in a terminal on the login node just by typing `python`.

- This is useful for setting up all the Python libraries you may need.
- You are not supposed to do heavy multi-core computing on the login node, but installing libraries and small tests is okay.
- For example, you can install packages using:

```bash
pip install --user numpy pandas statsmodels
```

then your subsequent Python jobs run via `sbatch` are able to use `import numpy` or `import statsmodels`.

## Other ways to run Python on greatlakes

It can be useful to start an interactive session on greatlakes, particularly for debugging. This is done from the terminal as follows:

```bash
module load python
srun --nodes=1 --account=datasci531w26_class \
  --ntasks-per-node=8 --pty /bin/bash
```

You can then run Python in the terminal as usual, by typing

```bash
python
```

- This Python session will have access to the cores you have requested.
- Here, we require `nodes=1` since `multiprocessing` alone cannot work with cores spread across different machines.
- You can also run [web-based Jupyter](https://documentation.its.umich.edu/node/4996). However, batch jobs remain the basic tool for intensive statistical computing.

## Acknowledgments

- This lesson builds on the [Great Lakes User Guide](https://its.umich.edu/advanced-research-computing/high-performance-computing/great-lakes), an [introduction by Charles Antonelli and John Thiels](https://ionides.github.io/531w20/cluster/STATS_531_Introduction_to_R_and_pomp_on_Great_Lakes.pdf), and notes from [STATS 810](https://ionides.github.io/810f25/gl/notes.pdf).

- Compiled on \today.
